story:
Alberto arrived at Leving Rum Station after the rain began, his mother’s pink lipstick still stamped on his forehead. He set down his rattling trunk and turned over his ticket: a crowned, purple octopus with eight coiling arms. A cinnamon-haired little girl with a pumpkin head tottered past with a peeling pink suitcase whose latch gave way, scattering snacks. A woman in an emerald uniform—matching Engine 19—swept down, gathered the debris, sang “Ticket please,” punched it with a silver punch, and checked a gold pocket watch. “Two minutes!” she called, then winked at Alberto and ushered him aboard.

The passenger car filled with cotton, polyester, and velour furs. Alberto found a seat beside the round-faced thumb-sucker from the platform. He tucked his trunk beneath and folded his hands. The conductor strode to the front and beamed. “Welcome to Fast-Track Frasier’s First-Class Railway. I’m Mrs. Anne Frasier, your conductor to the ever-delightful Siesta Village. Exits here, here, here, and here.” Tea at noon; chicken soup with goldfish crackers. “Ca-ckers peas!” the little girl crowed, earning a nod. “Keep hands, feet, arms, legs—and anything else you wish not to lose—inside at all times. In danger, hands on cheeks, mouth in an ‘o,’ brows high, scream ‘Ahhhh!’” Their shriek drew a blond mechanic who poked in, “Too loud, you guys.” Mrs. Frasier tossed him a look, kissed his cheek, blushed cotton-candy pink, then added, “Beware the giant octopi. They love snacks. Do not feed them.” She eyed the children. They giggled. “Don’t touch,” Alberto warned as the girl toyed with a loose string by the window. “You can rip the train.”

The whistle bellowed. The train lurched, chugged, and sometimes neighed. The girl sang Old McDonald, shoes off, socks half-peeled. “Beto, kiss?” “No, Mila, we’re pretending we don’t know each other.” “Doggy?” “No.” “Mommy!” The conductor popped back, alarmed. “Narrow tunnel ahead! Huddle close so we don’t crush any snacks.” Alberto flung an arm over Mila. Darkness wrapped them; the car groaned and squeezed, pressing cotton and polyester limbs like sardines. Light returned; passengers sprawled. Mrs. Frasier reappeared with a tea trolley: PB&J triangles, grapes, strawberries, and two special hot chocolates. Warmth slid into their bellies.

Then mischief sparked in Mila’s eyes. She lifted her bag of goldfish crackers and glanced at the lake winking outside. “Don’t do it, Mila!” But up rose eight tentacles, reaching toward the sun, pausing, then lashing the train. The children performed the drill—hands to cheeks, open mouths, high screams—as glittering confetti sprinkled the air. A one-eyed plush crocodile vanished into the ripples. Passengers bleated and flailed. A lamb with a bell nearly went under until Mila caught it. “Peas, Beto!” Alberto crawled for his trunk, dodging suckers. Inside: toy cars, tiny trains, a plastic hammer. He almost played—then a tentacle knocked off Mrs. Frasier’s hat, and her look steadied him. He whacked the tentacle gripping his shirt; it released him.

“Five minutes,” called the mechanic. “No, Daddy, no bed,” Mila mumbled. Mrs. Frasier softened. “Daddy’s right, sweetie.” Alberto yawned; Mila tugged the loose string, already dozing. Daddy lifted her from the train tent. “Five more minutes, Beto,” Daddy said. “Make them count.”

Mrs. Frasier brushed Alberto’s hair. “We’re almost at Siesta Village.” Heavy-lidded from hot chocolate and octopus battles, he let her scoop him up. “Train conductor, Mommy?” “Yes, bebé.” “I love you.” The emerald coach faded to canvas and cushions; the lake was a window, the platform a rug, the passengers stuffed friends. She kissed his forehead, leaving a fresh pink mark. “Welcome to Siesta Village, my love.”
